title: Der Kreis adds English-language section
description: Starting with issue 12 of 1951, the Zurich-based homophile magazine Der Kreis – Le Cercle carried a regular English-language section. From 1955 the publication appeared under the trilingual name Der Kreis – Le Cercle – The Circle, reflecting its international readership.

title: Barfüsser opens in Zurich's Niederdorf
description: In August 1956 the cellar bar Barfüsser opened in Zurich's Niederdorf quarter. It went on to serve as a popular gay and lesbian meeting place for many years.

title: Isola club venue opens in Basel
description: On 23 March 1957 the club venue Isola opened in Basel, giving the city's homophile community a meeting place of its own.

title: Der Kreis marks 25 years
description: The Zurich homophile organization Der Kreis celebrated its 25th anniversary in 1957, a quarter century after its beginnings in the early 1930s.

title: Murders of Obussier and Rusterholz trigger repression in Switzerland
description: In 1957 Robert Obussier and Ernst Rusterholz were murdered. The two killings became the pretext for mounting police repression against homosexual men in Switzerland.

title: Swiss police begin raids and registration of homosexuals
description: From 1958 police carried out raids and systematically registered homosexual men, first concentrated in Zurich and later extending to Basel, Bern and other Swiss cities.

title: Police repression in Switzerland peaks
description: Police repression of homosexual men in Switzerland reached its high point around 1960 and continued at intensity through 1969.

title: Zurich dance ban targets Der Kreis
description: In 1960 Der Kreis lost its club venue at the Eintracht / Theater am Neumarkt. Zurich's city council issued a dancing ban that applied exclusively to Der Kreis and its premises.

title: Aktion S syphilis-awareness campaign
description: In 1961 Der Kreis partnered with Prof. Schuppli, head of the dermatological university clinic in Basel, on Aktion S — a campaign informing gay men about syphilis and encouraging blood testing.

title: Motion in Swiss parliament seeks to recriminalize homosexual acts
description: On 5 December 1962 a motion in the Swiss National Council aimed to tighten the penal code so that homosexual acts, decriminalized in 1942, would again be punishable. The push did not become law.

title: Private gatherings in Bern cellar precede Ursus Club
description: In 1964 private gatherings began in a cellar in Bern. Out of these meetings the Ursus Club later developed.

title: Conti-Club opens as new Der Kreis venue in Zurich
description: On 5–6 February 1966 the Conti-Club opened at Köchlistrasse 15 in Zurich as the new club venue of Der Kreis. It served the organization until 1967 and was afterwards associated with Club 68 and the SOH.

title: Swiss-German television airs discussions on homosexuality
description: On 30 January and 20 February 1967, German-language Swiss television broadcast discussion programs on the theme of homosexuality, framed as public education on the subject.

title: Der Kreis votes to dissolve
description: At its final annual assembly on 22 October 1967, Der Kreis resolved to dissolve both its magazine and the organization itself, ending Europe's longest-running homophile publication of the postwar era.

title: Final issue of Der Kreis published
description: The last issue of the magazine Der Kreis appeared in December 1967, closing 35 years of continuous publication from Zurich.

title: Club 68 launches with its own magazine
description: On 10 December 1967 former subscribers of Der Kreis established the Club 68 association together with its German-language magazine club68, carrying the Swiss homophile movement forward after the dissolution of Der Kreis.

title: Ursus Club association forms in Bern
description: On 8 March 1968 the Ursus Club was formally constituted as an association in Bern, growing out of the private cellar gatherings that had begun in 1964.

title: Stonewall riots in New York
description: During a police raid on the Stonewall Inn on Christopher Street in New York, patrons fought back against police harassment. Six days of street clashes followed, now known as the Stonewall riots, widely regarded as the starting point of the modern gay liberation movement.

title: Conti-Club admits women
description: In January 1970 women were admitted to Zurich's Conti-Club for the first time, opening the formerly all-male venue to a mixed crowd.

title: First Christopher Street Day march in New York
description: On 28 June 1970 New York held the first Christopher Street Day march, commemorating the Stonewall riots of one year earlier. The march became the model for pride events worldwide.

title: Club 68 opens short-lived St. Gallen section
description: On 20 March 1971 Club 68 established a section in St. Gallen. The local branch survived only a few months.

title: Club 68 admits Geneva clubs, paving way to national SOH
description: On 3 April 1971 Club 68 admitted the Geneva clubs L'olivier and la calèche. This cleared the path for its transformation into a national body, the Schweizerische Organisation der Homophilen (SOH).

title: Club Zabi opens in Zurich
description: On 12 May 1971 the Club Zabi, named after Zabriskie Point, opened in Zurich.

title: Rosa von Praunheim's landmark gay-liberation film premieres in Berlin
description: On 3 July 1971 Rosa von Praunheim's film It Is Not the Homosexual Who Is Perverse, But the Society in Which He Lives premiered in Berlin. The provocative film became a catalyst for the gay liberation movement in German-speaking countries.

title: club68 magazine adds pages for women and French readers
description: In 1971 the magazine club68 introduced its first page for women, initially titled Sie and later Lady S, running until the end of the year. From April a regular French-language page appeared under the title Le courier romand.

title: Zabriskie Release counseling service starts in Zurich
description: In 1971 Zabriskie Release, a counseling service for homosexuals, was set up in Zurich.

title: Club In joins SOH as Ticino section
description: In 1972 the Club In of Lugano-Pregassona joined the Schweizerische Organisation der Homophilen, becoming the organization's Ticino section.

title: hey and PLÄDOYER succeed club68 magazine
description: In February 1972 two new publications, hey and PLÄDOYER, appeared as successor titles to the magazine club68.

title: Praunheim film reaches Swiss screens
description: On 12 March 1972 the film group DAF (Der Andere Film) gave Rosa von Praunheim's gay-liberation film its Swiss premiere in Zurich, followed by further screenings in Basel and Bern.

title: Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen Zürich (HAZ) founded
description: On 22 March 1972 activists in Zurich formed the association Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen Zürich - Zabriskie Point (HAZ - ZABI), known as HAZ. Its newsletter HAZinfo began appearing shortly afterwards. HAZ became a driving force of the new Swiss gay liberation movement.

title: Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen Basel (HAB) founded
description: On 27 June 1972 the Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen Basel formed, initially abbreviated HAB and later HABS. The group later published its own newsletter, habs-info.

title: Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen Bern (HAB) founded
description: On 6 December 1972 the Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen Bern formed, adding the Swiss capital to the growing network of gay liberation groups. Its newsletter HAB-Info appeared at irregular intervals.

title: HABS university group forms in Basel
description: On 14 February 1973 students in Basel formed the HABS-Unigruppe, a student branch of the city's gay liberation group.

title: Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen St. Gallen (HASG) founded
description: On 15 March 1973 the Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen St. Gallen formed, extending the Swiss gay liberation network to eastern Switzerland.

title: LOGE 70 forms for leather and fetish community
description: In May 1973 LOGE 70 (Schweiz) came into being as an association for leather- and fetish-oriented homosexual men in Switzerland.

title: PLÄDOYER ceases publication
description: In spring 1973 PLÄDOYER, a periodical for minorities that had emerged from club68 the year before, was discontinued.

title: First Boldern conference on homosexuality
description: On 26–27 January 1974 the Protestant conference center Boldern above Männedorf hosted the first conference on homosexuality, organized with the Catholic Paulus Akademie Zürich. The two church institutions went on to hold the conference annually.

title: Conti-Club closes in Zurich
description: In March 1974 Zurich's Conti-Club, which had opened in 1966 as the venue of Der Kreis, closed its doors.

title: ISOLA Club moves to Gempenstrasse in Basel
description: On 12 October 1974 the ISOLA Club in Basel relocated to new premises at Gempenstrasse 60, where it operated as a disco until 2003.

title: Groupe Symétrie forms in Lausanne
description: In autumn 1974 Le groupe Symétrie formed in Lausanne. From 1975 it acted as the local section of the Schweizerische Organisation der Homophilen.

title: HACH founded as national umbrella of Swiss gay liberation groups
description: On 14 December 1974 the Homosexuelle Arbeitsgruppen Schweiz (HACH) formed as the umbrella organization of all HA groups in Switzerland, uniting the country's gay liberation movement under one roof.

title: GLHOG forms in Geneva as FHAR offshoot
description: In 1975 the Groupe libertaire d'homosexuels genevois (GLHOG) formed in Geneva as an offshoot of the French Front Homosexuel d'Action Révolutionnaire. The group dissolved two years later.

title: Contact point for parents of gays and lesbians opens
description: In August 1976 a contact point for parents of gay and lesbian children opened in Kappel in the canton of Solothurn, offering families support at a time when few such services existed.

title: CLUB HEY opens in Zurich
description: On 22 December 1976, after a long period of preparation, the CLUB HEY opened in Zurich.

title: Groupe de Libération Homosexuelle forms in Lausanne
description: In 1976 the Groupe de Libération Homosexuelle (GLH) came together in Lausanne, adding to the gay liberation scene of French-speaking Switzerland.

title: Arcados, Switzerland's first gay bookshop, opens in Basel
description: On 1 April 1977 Peter Thommen opened Arcados in Basel, the first gay bookshop in Switzerland.

title: Special issue anderschume obelix appears
description: In May 1977 four HA groups jointly published the special issue anderschume obelix, a precursor to the movement's later periodical.

title: Groupe homosexuel de Genève forms
description: In February 1978 the Groupe homosexuel de Genève (GHOG) came together, giving Geneva a new gay liberation group after the demise of GLHOG.

title: First issue of anderschume aktuell published
description: In February 1978 the first issue of anderschume aktuell appeared, continuing the publishing efforts of the Swiss HA groups.

title: Swiss television broadcasts Telearena on homosexuality
description: On 12 April 1978 Swiss television aired an edition of the program Telearena devoted to homosexuality, bringing the topic to a broad national audience.
